Facts.text: The first Crusaders arrive in Antioch, Ancient Syria, during the First Crusade
Spanish troops occupy & plunder Maastricht
Battle at Coultras: Henry of Navarre's  Huguenot army defeats the Catholic League, led by Anne Duke of Joyeuse, who is killed trying to surrender
Battle of Sekigahara sets Tokugawa clan as Japan's rulers (shoguns)
Chinese uprising in the Philippines fails after 23,000 are killed
Great Fire of Copenhagen breaks out at the Vesterport (the West Gate), burns for three days destroying about a third of the city
Maria Theresa becomes ruler of Austria, Hungary & Bohemia
Royal ship Duc de Bourgogne launched at Rochefort
American Continental Congress orders discouragement of entertainment
Harvard University organizes 1st astronomical expedition in US
US Senate ratifies the Louisiana Purchase
German Kingdom of Westphalia abolished
1st Mississippi "Showboat" leaves Nashville on maiden voyage
49th parallel forms as border between US & Canada
US and Britain agree to joint control of Oregon country
1st edition of London Sunday Times
Naval Battle of Navarino (off the Peloponnese coast): English, Russian, French combined fleet beat a Turkish and Egyptian force, paving the way for Greek independence
HMS Beagle leaves Galapagos Archipelago to sail to Tahiti
1st Chinese immigrant arrives in Suriname
12-year-old English boy William Nelman poisons his grandpa with arsenic, would become famous court case
Arnhem-Oberhausen railway in Netherlands opens
Amnesty proclaimed for escaped slaves of Suriname
Amsterdam Free University opens
Max Bruch's "Kol Nidre" 1st performed
Treaty of Ancon; Peru cedes Tarapaca to Chile
Chicago and All America baseball teams play exhibition in Auckland, New Zealand
1st International 6-day bike race at New York City's Madison Square Garden begins
NC Mutual & Provident Insurance Company forms
American yacht Columbia beats the Shamrock challenge from Royal Ulster Yacht Club in 11th America's Cup
Battle at Talana Hill, Natal: British army vs Boers
The Chamber of Deputies appoints a committee to consider questions on the separation of Church and State in France
US wins disputed boundary between District of Alaska & Canada
Bolivia and Chile sign a treaty ending the War of the Pacific; recognizing Chile's possession of the coast, providing for construction of a railway linking La Paz, Bolivia, to Arica on the coast
Great General Strike in Russia begins; lasts 11 days
Dr Lee DeForest demonstrates his electrical vacuum tube (radio tube)
Soccer team KFC forms in Alkmaar
The hull of the RMS Olympic, sister-ship to the ill-fated RMS Titanic, launched from Harland and Wolff shipyard in Belfast, Ireland
Cort Theater opens at 148 W 48th St NYC
Hannes Kolehmainen runs world record marathon (2:29:39.2)
"1st Year" with Frank Craven premieres in NYC
Germany and Allies comes to an agreements over reparation payments in a meeting at Wiensbaden
The French and Mustafa Kemal nationalists sign a treaty at Ankara fixing the Turko-Syrian border
Kennelworth in Bronx renamed Dwight Place
Hurricane in Cuba, kills 600
Bayshore Highway opens (San Francisco)
Adventures of Sherlock Holmes, premieres on NBC radio
British White Paper restricts Jews from buying Arab land
Journalist Robert Trout joins CBS
400,000 demonstrators against fascism in Madrid
Anti-fascist People's Front forms in Brussels
Spanish government moves to Barcelona
Cheese rationed in the Netherlands
Greenhouse rationing begins in the Netherlands
Nazi occupiers murder 500 inhabitants of Kragujevac Serbia
"Durham Manifesto" issued by the Southern Conference on Race Relations held in Durham, North Carolina, calls for fundamental changes in race relations
Liquid-gas tanks in Cleveland, Ohio, explode, 135 die, and 3,600 are left homeless
Revolution by workers & students in Guatemala
Soviet and Yugoslav troops free Belgrade
US 1st army wins battle of Aachen on Germany's western border
Frank Seno returns kickoff 105 yd, Chicago Cards vs NY Giants
HUAC opens hearings into alleged Communist influence in Hollywood
Eugenie Anderson becomes 1st woman US ambassador (to Denmark)
The "Johnny Bright Incident" occurs during a football game in Stillwater, Oklahoma; African-American Quaterback from Drake College suffers a broken jaw in a violent attack by Oklahoma A&M defenders
Emergency crisis proclaimed in Kenya
WRAU (now WHOI) TV channel 19 in Peoria, IL (ABC) begins broadcasting
NY Yankees begin a 16-game exhibition in Japan
58°F (15°C), Esperanza Station, Antarctica (then Antarctic record high)
Hannes Lindemann begins journey across Atlantic in a 17' craft
Karachi A (277-0d) beat Sind A by an innings without losing a wicket
WABG TV channel 6 in Greenwood-Greenville, MS (ABC) 1st broadcast
1st fully mechanized post office opened, Providence, Rhode Island
Chinese army lands in India
US performs atmospheric nuclear test at Johnston Island
France performs underground nuclear test at Ecker, Algeria
WITV TV channel 7 in Charleston, South Carolina (PBS) begins broadcasting
Ann Packer of Great Britain runs a world record 2:01.1 to win the women's 800m gold medal at the Tokyo Olympics
Mad Dog Vachon beats Verne Gagne in Minneapolis, to become NWA champ
Riot at a Rolling Stones concert in Paris leads to 150 arrests
Tamara Press of the Soviet Union wins her second gold medal in 2 days by taking out the women's shot put at the Tokyo Olympics; Press' second consecutive Olympic shot put title
Beatles receive a gold record for the single "Yesterday"
Mass arrests of communists in Indonesia
A purported bigfoot is filmed at Bluff Creek by Roger Patterson and Robert Gimlin in Northern California
All white federal jury convicts 7 in murder of 3 civil rights workers in Meridan Mississippi
KMXN (now KJTV) TV channel 34 in Lubbock, TX (IND) begins broadcasting
Owner Charlie Finley names Bob Kennedy 1st manager of the newly relocated Oakland A's
American Dick Fosbury using his unconventional technique wins the men's high jump gold medal with 2.24m at the Mexico City Olympics; "Fosbury Flop" becomes accepted most efficient technique
Kenyan runner Kip Keino wins 1,500m gold medal at the Mexico City Olympics in 3:34.91 despite a severe gall bladder infection
Mamo Wolde wins 16th Olympic marathon (2:20:26.4)
Margitta Gummel of East Germany throws world record 19.61m to beat teammate Marita Lange by 0.83m and win the women's shot put gold medal at the Mexico City Olympics
US men's 4 x 400m relay team of Vincent Matthews, Ron Freeman, Larry James & Lee Evans runs world record 2:56.16; easily beats Kenya & West Germany into the minor medals at the Mexico City Olympics
US women's 4 x 100m relay team sets world record 42.88s to win the gold medal at the Mexico City Olympics; Margaret Bailes, Barbara Ferrell, Mildrette Netter & Wyomia Tyus
WKYH (now WYMT) TV channel 57 in Hazard, KY (NBC) begins broadcasting
American agronomist Norman Borlaug awarded the Nobel Peace Prize for his contribution to world food supply
Zond 8 Launches to orbit the Moon
The Nepal stock exchange collapses.
The Family Station Inc buys shortwave Radio Station WNYW, changes call letters to WYFR & moves station from NYC to Scituate, Massachusetts

US President Nixon accepts the resignations of Attorney General Elliot Richardson and Deputy Attorney General William Ruckelshaus as they refuse orders to discharge Watergate Special Prosecutor Archibald Cox, in what has become known as "The Saturday Night Massacre"; after the resignations, Acting Attorney General Robert Bork fires Cox
1st broadcast of "Derrick" on ZDF
Bard's presentation of "Richard III" opens at Lincoln Center NYC
Soviet orbiter Venera 9 becomes the 1st spacecraft to orbit Venus
Supreme Court rules teachers could spank their pupils after warning
George Prince ferry disaster: 70 die when Norwegian tanker SS Frosta collides with the George Prince ferry on the Mississippi River in Louisiana
NY Nets Julius "Dr J" Erving sold to Philadelphia 76ers

Plane chartered by rock band Lynyrd Skynyrd runs out of fuel and crashes in a wooded area near Gillsburg, Mississippi. Six people are killed, including band members Ronnie Van Zant, Steve Gains, and Cassie Gaines, and road manager Dean Kilpatrick
Revival of stage play "Dracula" premieres in NYC starring Frank Langella as Dracula
British ska-rock band The Police perform 1st US concert at CBGBs in NYC
Paul Vanden Boeynants forms Belgium government
US dollar devalued below Dutch Ÿ2
American John Tate beats local favourite Gerrie Coetzee by UD in 15 rounds in Pretoria, South Africa for vacant WBA heavyweight boxing title
1st NBA game at Meadowlands Arena, NJ Nets lose to NY Knicks 103-99
3 members of Weather underground arrested for armored truck robbery
Bomb attack on Antwerp Belgium synagogue, 1 dead, 80 injured
Nobel Prize in Economics awarded to George Stigler
Sri Lanka President Jayewardene re-elected
IBM-PC DOS Version 2.1 released
Cleveland Metroparks' Valley Parkway All Purpose Trails are completed
The Monterey Bay Aquarium opens in Monterey Bay, California.
Tupolev-134 crashes in Southern Africa
10 die as US Air Force jet crashed into a Ramada Inn near Indianapolis
Dow Jones Industrial Average increases 102.27 pts/608,120,000 shares traded (record)
Subway vigilante Bernhard Goetz sentenced to 6 months in jail
Britain ends suspects' right to remain silent in crackdown on IRA
Gastineau sacks Jets, retires from football "for personal reasons"
Man armed with explosives blows himself up in 125 St subway station (NYC)
Reggie Rogers, Det Lion's # 1 pick, kills 3 by driving intoxicated
Pakistan win Sharjah Trophy over India & WI on round-robin
US Senate impeaches US District Judge Alcee L Hastings
3 members of 2 Live Crew acquitted on obsenity charges in Florida
Antiwar protest marches begin in 20 US cities (US-Iraq)
6.1-7.1 earthquake in Uttar Kashi, India, about 670 die
Formal opening ceremony of Intl One Mind Zen center in Crestone, Colo
David Houghton scores Zimbabwe's 1st Test hundred (121 v India, debut)
Mr Johnson surrenders Monrovia, Liberia & is exiled to Nigeria
Sri Lanka beat West Indies to win Sharjah Champions Trophy final
STS 73 (Columbia 18) launches into orbit
Richard Gnida, Limo driver in Detroit Red Wings crash, pleads guilty
US accuses Microsoft of violating pact forcing IE browser on computers
6th Rugby World Cup Final, Stade de France, Saint-Denis: Springboks fullback Percy Montgomery lands 4/4 penalties as South Africa beats England, 15-6
"Water by the Spoonful" play by Quiara Alegría Hudes opens at the Hartford Stage (Pulitzer Prize for Drama 2012)
30 people are killed by a suicide truck bomber in Hama, Syria
37 people are killed in a suicide bombing in Baghdad, Iraq
78 people are killed by cattle raiders in Jonglei, Sudan
Laquan McDonald (17) is shot dead by Chicago police officer Jason Van Dyke while jaywalking, the murder caught on video footage
Migrants arriving in Greece top 500,00 for the year, according to the UN
Treaty of Gorinchem signed between Holy Roman EmperorCharles Vand Duke Charles of Guelders
KingChristian IIIof Denmark & Norway leads reform in Catholic possessions
King of EnglandCharles Ilevies the "Ship Money" tax on all English and Welsh counties
Georg Ludwig von Hannover crowned as Britain's KingGeorge Iat Westminster Abbey
Margaret Fulleris appointed editor of new US Transcendental Magazine "The Dial"
Franz Schubert's 2nd Symphony in B premieres in London, England by Crystal Palace Orchestra, conducted by Alfred Manns
Gerhart Hauptmann's play "Vor Sonnenaufgang" (Before Dawn) premieres in Berlin to a scandalous reception
Russian TsarNicholas IIallows the Poles to speak Polish to help quell the revolution in the Kingdom of Poland
KingLeopold IIsells Congo to Belgium
Helen HayesTheater (Folies Bergere) opens at 210W 46th St, NYC
NorwegianRoald Amundsensets out a race to the South Pole
US suffragetteAlice Paulbegins a seven-month jail sentence for peacefully picketing in support of the Women's Suffrage (right to vote) Amendment at the White House in Washington, D.C.
Wien Alaska Airways, Inc. incorporated; the first airline in Alaska and one of the 1st in the US withNoel Wienas president
Frankie Frischof the Cards named MVP
MLB All-Star team led by Connie Mack and includingBabe Ruth,Jimmie FoxxandLou Gehrigsails to Japan for 18-game series against Big-Six University League
Richard Strausscompletes his opera "Die Schweigsame Frau" (The Silent Woman)
Communist forces end their Long March at Yan'an, in Shaanxi, China, bringingMao Zedongto prominence
Detroit Tigers future Baseball Hall of Fame first basemanHank Greenbergis named AL MVP by the BWAA; Boston Red Sox pitcherWes Ferrellis runner-up
Carl Hubbell, 26-6, edges out Dizzy Dean, 24-13, for MVP honors in NL
"All the Things You Are" recorded byTommy DorseyOrchestra
PopePius XIIpublishes his 1st encyclical Summi pontificatus
Robinson Jeffers' play "Medea" adapted fromEuripidespremieres in NYC

ChemistsArcher John Porter Martinand James Lovelock give the first demonstration of gas chromatography at a meeting of the Biochemical Society. The technique is rapidly adopted by the petrochemical industry.
"The Return of the King," the third and final volume of "TheLord of the Rings" byJ. R. R. Tolkien, is published by George Allen & Unwin in London
Calypso singerHarry Belafonterecords his signature tune "Day-O" (Banana Boat Song) in NYC
Walter Cronkitebegins hosting weekly documentary
Clark Griffithof Senators says team will not move the franchise
Ralph Houk, 41, replacesCasey Stengelas Yankee manager
Peter, Paul and Mary's debut folk album "Peter, Paul and Mary" reaches No. 1 on US album charts
Alec Douglas-Home's Conservative Party forms British government
Jim Brownsets NFL single-season rushing record, 1,863 yds
South Africa begins trial ofNelson Mandela& 8 others on conspiracy charges
Nobel Prize for Literature awarded jointly to Jewish writers Shmuel Yosef Agnon andNelly Sachs

American sprinterJim Hinesanchors US men's 4 x 100m relay team to win his second gold medal at the Mexico City Olympics; with teammates Charles Greene, Mel Pender & Ronnie Ray Smith sets world record 38.24s
US SenatorEdward Kennedycalls for a withdrawal of British troops from Northern Ireland and all-party negotiations to establish a United Ireland
West German ChancellorWilly Brandtis awarded Nobel Peace Prize
John Betjeman is appointed British Poet Laureate byQueen Elizabeth II
Queen Julianavisits Yugoslavia
Mariette Hartley appears on "TheBob NewhartShow" in the episode "Have You Met Miss Dietz?"
US President Nixon proclaimsJim Thorpeto have been the greatest athlete of the 1st half of the 20th century
David Mamet's "Life in the Theater" premieres in NYC
Bob Dylanmake his only appearance on "Saturday Night Live" performing three songs from his album Slow Train Coming: ‘When You Gonna Wake Up’, ‘Gotta Serve Somebody’ and ‘I Believe in You’'
John F. KennedyLibrary dedicated in Boston, Massachusetts
Geffen Records release their first album,Donna Summer's "The Wanderer"
Billy Martinfired as manager of Oakland A's
Islander'sMike Bossy's 30th career hat trick, in his 6th career 4-goal game, an 8-3 win over visiting Los Angeles Kings
Brazilian McLaren driverAyrton Sennaclinches his 2nd straight, and 3rd overall F1 World Drivers Championship by finishing 2nd in the Japanese Grand Prix at Suzuka
Wasim Akram(257) & Saqlain Mushtaq gets cricket Test record 313 for 8th wicket, vs Zimbabwe at Sheikhupura
ComedianRichard Pryorreceives the first-ever Kennedy Center Mark Twain Prize for American Humor
Motoring TV series "Top Gear" premieres on the BBC, starringJeremy Clarkson,Richard Hammond, and Jason Dawe (replaced after the first season byJames May)
The former leader of Libya,Muammar Gaddafi, and his son Moatassem Gaddafi are killed shortly after the battle of Sirte (2011) while in the custody of NTC fighters.
Joko Widodobecomes President of Indonesia
US Vice PresidentJoe Bidenconfirms he will not run for President in 2016
In order to secure a WWI armistice, Germany agrees to further concessions
US forces under GeneralDouglas MacArthurreturn to the Philippines with the landing of the US 6th army on Leyte
Queen Elizabeth IIopens the Sydney Opera House at Bennelong Point in Sydney, Australia, after 14 years of construction
Andrea Della Robbia, Italian sculptor, nephew of Luca, born in Florence  (d. 1525)
Giovanni Rucellai, Italian humanist and poet (The Bees  - one of the 1st famous poems in blank verse), born in Florence (d. 1525)
Claude de Lutherans, 1st duke of Guise, French general (Battle of Marignano), born in Condé-sur-Moselle, France (d. 1550)
Thomas Bartholin, Danish physician, mathematician, and theologian, discovered the lymphatic system, born in Malmø, Sweden (d. 1680)
Aelbert Cuyp, Dutch Golden Age painter, chiefly of landscapes and riverscapes (Cows in a River), born in Dordrecht, Dutch Republic (d. 1691)
Nicolaas van der Veken, Flemish sculptor (confessional chairs), born in Mechelen (d. 1709)
Nicolas de Largillière, French painter, born in Paris, France (d. 1746)
Robert Bertie, 1st Duke of Ancaster and Kesteven, English statesman, born in England (d. 1723)

Stanislaus I [Leszczyński], Polish regent (King of Poland and Grand Duke of Lithuania, 1704-09, King of Poland, 1733-36; Duke of Lorraine and Bar, 1737-66), born in Lwów, Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth (d. 1766)ST
Samuel von Cocceji, German lawyer, Grand Chancellor of Prussia, born in Heidelberg (d. 1755)
Timothy Ruggles, American leading Tory politician who supported the British during the American Revolutionary War, born in Rochester, Massachusetts (d. 1795)
Gottfried Achenwall, German lawyer, pioneering statistician and economist, born in Elbing, Prussia (d. 1772)
Belle van Zuylen, Dutch-Swiss writer (Three Women), born at Castle Zuylen near Utrecht (d. 1805)
Chauncey Goodrich, American politician, U.S. Senator from Connecticut, born in Durham, Connecticut Colony (d. 1815)
Pauline Bonaparte, Corsican duchess of Parma and Guastalla, sister of Napoleon Bonaparte, born in Ajaccio, Corsica (d. 1825)
George Ormerod, English historian and antiquarian, born in Manchester, England (d. 1873)
Philip Knapton, English composer and organist (Caller Herrin), born in York, England (d. 1833)
Anton Bernhard Furstenau, German composer and flutist, born in Münster, Prince-Bishopric of Münster Holy Roman Empire, (d. 1852)
Colin Campbell, Lord Clyde, British army officer (Opium Wars, Sepoy-uprising), born in Glasgow, Scotland (d. 1863)
Karl Andree, German geographer and journalist (founded journal Globus), born in Braunschweig, Saxony (d. 1875)
Austin Flint, American physician and heart research pioneer (Austin heart murmur), born in Petersham, Massachusetts (d. 1886)
Daniel E. Sickles, American lawyer, politician (US Representative from New York, 1857-61 and 1893-95), diplomat, and Civil War Major General (Union Army), born in New York City (d. 1914)[1]
Karol Mikuli, Polish composer, pianist, and friend of Frederic Chopin, born in Czerniowice, Bukovina  (now Chernivtsi, Ukraine) (d. 1897)
The Báb, Persian founder of the Bábí Faith, born in Shiraz, Iran (d. 1850)
Benjamin Franklin Cheatham, American Major General in the Confederate Army (let General John M. Schofield escape), born in Nashville, Tennessee (d. 1886)
George Jerrison Stannard, American farmer, teacher, governmental official and Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Georgia, Vermont (d. 1886)
Mansfield Lovell, American Major General in Confederate Army (Commander when New Orleans fell), born in the Dictrict of Columbia (d. 1884)
Thomas Hughes, English politician and author (Tom Brown's School Days), born in Uffington (d. 1896)
Constantin Lipsius, German architect (Lipsius-Bau), born in Leipzig (d. 1894)
Victor de Stuers, Dutch art historian (saved Johannes Vermeer's "Girl with a Pear Earring"), born in Maastricht, Netherlands (d. 1916)
Alphonse Allais, French humorist and author (Pass the Bile), born in Honfluer, France (d. 1905)
John Burns, English politician and trade Unionist, born in London, England (d. 1943)
George Studd, English cricket batsman (4 Tests; Middlesex), born in Netheravon, Wiltshire, England (d. 1945)
James F. Hinkle, American politician, sixth Governor of New Mexico, born in Franklin County, Missouri (d. 1951)
Fini Henriques, Danish composer and violinist (Vølund Smed), born in Frederiksberg, Denmark (d. 1940)
Charles Frederick Goldie, New Zealand painter, known for his portraits of Maori, born in Auckland, New Zealand (d. 1947)[1]
Frederick Burton, American actor (The Big Trail, One Way Passage), born in Indianapolis, Indiana (d. 1957)
Josephine McGill, American composer and folksong collector, born in Louisville, Kentucky (d. 1919)
Thomas Chalmers, American opera singer, actor, and filmmaker (Blind Alleys, Outrage), born in New York City (d. 1966)
Yasuhiko Asaka, Japanese Imperial Prince (1906-47) and Imperial Japanese Army commander (WWII), born in Kyōto, Japan (d. 1981)
Margaret Dumont, American actress and Marx Brothers' comic foil (Day at the Races), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1965)
Ferdinand "Jelly Roll" Morton [LeMothe], American jazz pioneer pianist and composer (King Porter Stomp), born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 1941)
Charley Chase, American comedian, actor and director, known for such silent shorts as "Mighty Like a Moose", born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 1940)
Olive Thomas, American actress (The Flapper), born in Charleroi, Pennsylvania (d. 1920)
Rex Ingram, American actor (The Adventures of Huckleberry Finn, Tarzan of the Apes, The Ten Commandments), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 1969)
Yevsei Liberman, Ukrainian Soviet economist (Plan, profit, bonuses), born in Slavuta, Russia (d. 1983)
Yi Un of Korea, last crown prince of Korea, born at Deoksu Palace, Seoul (d. 1970)
Evelyn Brent, American actress (Panama Lady, Last Command), born in Tampa, Florida (d. 1975)
Marnix Gijsen [Jan-Albert Goris], Belgian writer (Grote God Pan), born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1984)
Rodolfo Halffter, Spanish composer, and music critic, born in Madrid, Spain (d. 1987)
Wayne Morse, American politician (Senator-(R&D)-Oregon 1945-69), born in Madison, Wisconsin (d. 1974)
Adelaide Hall, American-English musical theatre, cabaret, and jazz singer (Cotton Club; "Creole Love Call" with Duke Ellington), born in Brooklyn, NYC (d. 1993)[1]
Hans-Otto Borgmann, German film score composer under the Third Reich, born in Linden, Germany (d. 1977)
Addison Richards, American actor (Pentagon, Fighting Seabees), born in Zanesville, Ohio (d. 1964)
John Lodge, American actor (Witchmaker) and politician (79th Governor of Connecticut), born in Washington, D.C. (d. 1985)
Anna Neagle, English actress (London Melody, Nurse Edith Cavell), born in London, England (d. 1986)
Marian Nixon [Marja Nissinen], American silent and sound film actress (Hands Up!; Rebecca of Sunnybrook Farms; Winner Takes All), born in Superior, Wisconsin (d. 1983)
Arlene Francis, American actress and radio and television personality (What's My Line?), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 2001)
Stuart Hamblen, American singer and composer (This Ole House), born in Kellyville, Texas (d. 1989)
Sugiyama Yasushi, Japanese Nihonga style painter, and designer, born in Asakusa, (d. 1993)
Bob Sheppard, American public address announcer (MLB: NY Yankees 1951–2007; NFL: NY Giants 1956–2006), born in Richmond Hill, New York (d. 2010)
Will Rogers Jr, American politician (D-Representative 1943-44) writer, political commentator, and actor (Down to Earth), born in New York City (d. 1993)
Angelo Ephrikian, Armenian-Italian violinist, conductor, and musicologist (early Italian music), born in Treviso, Italy (d. 1982)
Barney Phillips [Bernard Philip Ofner], American actor (Dragnet, Felony Squad), born in St Louis, Missouri (d. 1982)
Grandpa Jones, American country music singer and banjo wizard (Hee-Haw), born in Niagra, Kentucky (d. 1998)
Johnny Best, American big band jazz and session trumpeter (Artie Shaw; Glenn Miller), born in Shelby, North Carolina (d. 2003)
Fayard Nicholas, American tap dancer, one-half of The Nicholas Brothers and actor (The Five Heartbeats), born in Mobile, Alabama (d. 2006)
Giora Schuster, German-Israeli composer, born in Hamburg, German Empire (d. 2006)
Anton Diffring [Alfred Pollack], German character actor (Assignment Vienna), born in Koblenz, Germany (d. 1989)
Efrain Jonckheer, Premier of the Dutch Antilles, born in Curaçao (d. 1987)
Jean-Pierre Melville, French filmmaker (A Cop), born in Paris, France (d. 1973)
Ken Cranston, English cricket all-rounder (8 Tests, 18 wickets, BB 4/12; Lancashire CCC), born in Liverpool, England (d. 2007)
Robert Lochner, German journalist (help JFK with "Ich bin ein Berliner" speech), born in New York City (d. 2003)
Tracy Hall, American inventor (grew synthetic diamond), born in Ogden, Utah (d. 2008)
Dov Noy, Polish-born Jewish scholar of Jewish folklore, born in Kolomyia, Poland (d. 2013)[1]
Hans Warren, Dutch writer, poet and critic (Secret Diary), born in Borssele, Netherlands (d. 2001)
Pierre Laporte, Canadian journalist and politician (kidnapped and murdered while Vice-Premier of Quebec), born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 1970)
John Anderson, American actor (Psycho, Ride the High Country, Legend of Wyatt Earp), born in Clayton, Illinois (d. 1992)
Herschel Bernardi, American actor (Arnie, Voice of Charlie the Tuna, Front), born in New York City (d. 1986)
Joe Minogue, British journalist (Guardian), and D-Day army engineer, born in Salford, England (d. 1996)[1]
Robert Craft, American conductor, writer and friend of Stravinsky, born in Kingston, New York (d. 2015)
Kenneth W. Gatland, British pioneering aerospace scientist, born in the United Kingdom (d. 1997)
Stanley Booth-Clibborn, British Anglican Bishop of Manchester, born in London, England (d. 1996)
Art Buchwald, American columnist and author (Have I Ever Lied to You), born in Mt Vernon, New York (d. 2017)
Herman Roelstraete, Belgium composer, born in Lauwe, Belgium (d. 1985)
Roger Hanin, French actor (Navarro), born in Algiers, Algeria (d. 2015)
Theodore Hall, American Physicist and atomic spy for the Soviet Union, born in New York City (d. 1999)
Tom Dowd, American recording engineer and producer (innovator of multitrack recording), born in Manhattan, New York City (d. 2002)
Ursula Happe, German swimmer (Olympic gold 200m backstroke 1956), born in Gdańsk, Poland (d. 2021)
Joyce Brothers, American pop psychologist ($64,000 Question, Naked Gun), born in New York City (d. 2013)
Oskar Pastior, Romanian-born German writer (Oulipo), born in Sibiu (d. 2006)
Li Peng, Chinese leader, Premier of China (1987-98), born in Shanghai French Concession (d. 2019)
Jules Eskin, American cellist (Boston Symphony, 1964-2016), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 2016)
Ken Morrison, English supermarket tycoon (Morrisons), born in Bradford, England (d. 2017)
Zeke Bratkowski, American football quarterback (Super Bowl I, II; NFL C'ship 1965–67; Green Bay Packers HOF), born in Danville, Illinois (d. 2019)
"Rosey" Roosevelt Brown, American NFL hall of fame tackle (NY Giants), born in Charlottesville, Virginia (d. 2004)
William Christopher, American comedian and actor (M*A*S*H & AfterMASH - "Father Mulcahy"), born in Evanston, Illinois (d. 2016)
Barrie Chase, American dancer and actress (Cape Fear (1962); It's a Mad, Mad, Mad, Mad World), born in Kings Point, New York
Eddie Harris, American jazz saxophonist and composer ("Freedom Jazz Dance"), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1996)
Michael Dunn, American actor (House of the Damned, Ship of Fools), born in Shattuck, Oklahoma (d. 1973)
Michiko, Empress of Japan (1989-2019) by marriage to Emperor Akihito and the first commoner to marry into the Japanese royal family, born in Tokyo, Japan
Timothy West, English actor (Brass), born in Bradford, England
Jerry Orbach, American actor (Law & Order, Dirty Dancing), born in The Bronx, New York (d. 2004)
Joanna Simon, American mezzo-soprano (1962-86), and Emmy Award-winning arts journalist (PBS, 1986-92), born in New York City (d. 2022)[1]
Emma Tennant, English writer (Pemberley: or, Pride and Prejudice Continued), born in London (d. 2017)
Wanda Jackson, American country singer ("Let's Have a Party"), born in Maud, Oklahoma
Iain Macmillan, Scottish Abbey Road photographer, born in Carnoustie, Scotland (d. 2006)
Jay Siegel, American rock bassist (Tokens - "The Lion Sleeps Tonight"), born in Brooklyn, New York City
Raymond Jones, British rock bassist (Billy J. Kramer and the Dakotas), born in Oldham, England (d. 2000)
Kathy Kirby, English singer ("Secret Love"), born in Ilford, England (d. 2011)
Robert Pinsky, American poet and Poet Laureate of the United States, born in Long Branch, New Jersey
Earl Hindman, American actor (Home Improvement - "Wilson"; Ryan's Hope, 1975-89 - "Bob Reid"), born in Bisbee, Arizona (d. 2003)
Joe Bourne, American jazz and gospel singer ("Upbeat and Sweet"), and visual artist, born in Cambridge, Massachusetts
Dunja Vejzović, Croatian soprano, born in Zagreb, Croatia
Clive Hornby, British soap opera actor (Jack Sugden in "Emmerdale"), born in Liverpool, England (d. 2008)
William Hugh Albright, American composer and musician, born in Gary, Indiana (d. 1998)
Ric Lee, British blues-rock drummer (Ten Years After - "Love Like a Man"), born in Mansfield, England
Thomas Pasatieri, American opera composer (Frau Margot; Black Widow), born in New York City
Elfriede Jelinek, Austrian novelist, poet, and playwright (Nobel Prize for Literature, 2004 -The Piano Teacher), born in Mürzzuschlag, Austria
Lucien Van Impe, Belgian road cyclist (Tour de France General classification 1976; mountains classification x 6), born in Mere, Belgium
Bernard Lapasset, French rugby union executive (Chairman World Rugby 2008-16; President French Federation of Rugby Union 1991-2008), born in Tarbes, France (d. 2023)
Younis Ahmed, cricketer (batted for Pakistan in 1969 & again in 1987), born in Jalandhar, India
